policy_statement: Support Excess SLC Food Recovery to divert consumable food waste from landfill to food assistance programs.
mechanism_description: A partnership diverts consumable food waste from the landfill to food assistance programs, avoiding landfill disposal and reducing methane emissions from decomposing organics.
primary_causal_pathway: Waste handling choice (food recovery/diversion) → ↓ landfill organic deposition → ↓ anaerobic decomposition → ↓ CH₄ emissions → ↓ net GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Collection and routing of surplus edible food is shifted from disposal (landfill) to recovery and redistribution via food assistance programs.  
2) Mediating process: Reduced mass of biodegradable organics entering landfill cells lowers anaerobic breakdown and methane generation (and associated fugitive emissions), especially where gas capture is incomplete.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower methane (and some CO₂) emissions from the solid-waste sector, reducing overall GHG emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoided methane emissions by keeping organic (food) waste out of landfills where it would decompose anaerobically.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: food diversion from landfill → reduced anaerobic decomposition → reduced methane → reduced GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While food recovery has co-benefits (reduced upstream production if it displaces new food, social resilience via food security), the mechanism as stated explicitly targets landfill diversion and methane avoidance, which is a direct mitigation pathway.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a program primarily increases logistics energy use or does not actually reduce landfill-bound organics (e.g., rebound or double counting), net benefits could vary—but the canonical mechanism remains methane avoidance via diversion.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy using this mechanism—diverting edible food away from landfill disposal into recovery channels—shares the same primary climate effect of avoided landfill methane.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether the policy also delivers notable resource-efficiency benefits (upstream avoided production) or adaptation/social resilience co-benefits depends on local evidence and implementation details and should be assessed at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Net GHG benefit depends on baseline landfill gas capture rates, the counterfactual fate of the food (would it have been composted/AD anyway), and whether recovered food displaces new food production; increased transport/refrigeration emissions can reduce but rarely negate methane-avoidance benefits.
key_indicators: “partnership between community organizations”; “diverts consumable food waste from the landfill”; “to food assistance programs”
co_benefits: Improved food security/hunger relief; reduced disposal costs; community/public health benefits
instance_edge_case_notes: Classified as an incentive-program because the instrument is described as a partnership/program rather than a regulation or standard; the text does not specify whether funding/grants are provided, but the operational diversion program is clear.
